attributes.stem: Global climate data shows land temperatures 1.1°C above the 20th century average, ocean temperatures 0.66°C higher, and sea levels rising at 3.5 ± 0.4 mm/year.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the projected health impact of climate change without mitigation?
options: Causing regionally variable but globally minimal morbidity
Primarily affecting respiratory and cardiovascular conditions
Becoming the leading global cause of environmental disease
Creating transient disruptions to existing disease patterns
Generating health impacts proportional to proximity to the equator
attributes.explanation:
The correct answer is that climate change is projected to be becoming the leading global cause of environmental disease in the 21st century without immediate intervention. This reflects the severity and widespread nature of climate change impacts on human health through multiple pathways including extreme weather events, changes in infectious disease patterns, food and water insecurity, and displacement.

The incorrect options can be explained:

  • Causing regionally variable but globally minimal morbidity - This underestimates the projected global impact of climate change, which is expected to cause significant morbidity and mortality worldwide through various mechanisms including heat stress, malnutrition, and changing infectious disease patterns.
  • Primarily affecting respiratory and cardiovascular conditions - Although climate change does impact respiratory and cardiovascular health through increased air pollution, heat stress, and allergens, its health effects are much broader, affecting vector-borne diseases, nutrition, mental health, and many other aspects of human health.
  • Creating transient disruptions to existing disease patterns - Climate change is causing persistent and progressive alterations to disease patterns rather than temporary disruptions, with long-term shifts in vector ranges, food production, and water availability.
  • Generating health impacts proportional to proximity to the equator - Climate change affects all geographic regions, not just equatorial areas. Arctic regions are experiencing some of the most rapid warming, and temperate regions face increasing extreme weather events.

The data presented shows significant warming of both land (1.1°C above 20th century average) and ocean temperatures (0.66°C above 20th century average), along with measurable sea level rise (13-20 cm since 1900). These changes are driving multiple environmental disruptions including altered precipitation patterns, extreme weather events, and ecosystem changes that collectively contribute to the emergence of climate change as a dominant environmental health threat.
learningPoints: Climate change is projected to become the leading global cause of environmental disease in the 21st century.
Global temperatures have risen significantly, with land temperatures 1.1°C above the 20th century average in 2018.
Sea levels have risen 13-20 cm since 1900 and continue to rise at a rate of 3.5 ± 0.4 mm per year.
Climate change impacts health through multiple pathways including extreme weather events, altered disease patterns, and food insecurity.
references: Kumar V, Abbas AK, Aster JC. Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Philadelphia: Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 9; page 406-407.
